question_text:
Validate Binary Search Tree

Given the root of a binary tree, determine if it is a valid binary search tree (BST).

A valid BST is defined as follows:

  • The left subtree of a node contains only nodes with keys strictly less than the node's key.
  • The right subtree of a node contains only nodes with keys strictly greater than the node's key.
  • Both the left and right subtrees must also be binary search trees.
input_format: A single line of space-separated integers representing the binary tree in level-order (BFS) format. Use -1 to denote null nodes.
output_format: Print "true" if it is a valid BST, else "false".
constraints: The number of nodes in the tree is in the range [1, 10^4].
explanation: The critical insight is that a node in a BST must be strictly within an allowed range (min, max), not just greater than its left child and less than its right child. We perform a DFS with a valid range for each node: when going left, the upper bound becomes the parent's value; when going right, the lower bound becomes the parent's value. A node is invalid if its value is not strictly within the current range. This correctly handles cases like a right-subtree node being wrongly greater than the root. O(N) time, O(H) space where H is the tree height.
